full_transcript.speaker: Rahul Sood

full_transcript.text: And then I think about the opportunities. Like look at the opportunities—like a realtor, for example. That is a great opportunity.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: opportunity and that's the thing. And that's...

Or like the Flash comes and you're like, I own a pizzeria, and like the Flash shows up and he's like, what can I do? And it's like, you can deliver these pizzas. Vhoop! Pizzas are delivered. full_transcript.speaker: Rahul Sood

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Lon, Lukas, you don't have to hyperventilate, just talk.

full_transcript.text: Yeah, I will say that, you know, I have a lot of friends from university that went into being software developers, software engineers at the Magnificent Seven. full_transcript.speaker: Oliver Korzen
full_transcript.text: If you can architect, you can see the big picture, you can understand, like the mental model, you can build a mental model of the business and like what matters. Can you then creatively come up with ways to expand, grow, or otherwise improve the business and its products and services? So now the creative inherit the earth, right? The creative and the brave. That's it. Like, I think those are the skill sets for the future. Like are you self-possessed? Do you have like the the executive function to wake up every day and say how do I improve this business? Uh, and then doggedly improve the business with these systems and and building tools and services.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: My god, this has been another amazing episode of Twist. On Monday, we're going to do a review of all the different skills we can find. Best of Clawbot skills. We'll see you next time. Bye-bye.

highlights: AI orchestration platforms allow businesses to create "replicants" or autonomous agents that function as 24/7 digital employees capable of managing complex, recursive workflows like research and scheduling.

Early adoption of these autonomous agents can reduce the time spent on administrative tasks by 40% to 50%, shifting the human workload from rote execution to high-level creative and strategic oversight.

Integrating AI agents into core business systems introduces significant security vulnerabilities, such as "prompt injection" through external communications and the risk of malware embedded in third-party plugins or "skills.".

The widespread use of agentic AI is expected to permanently displace middle-management and administrative roles, making "system thinking" and the ability to architect AI workflows the most valuable skills in the labor market.

To manage high API token costs and improve data privacy, businesses are transitioning toward running large language models locally on high-performance hardware rather than relying exclusively on cloud-based providers.
summary:
This transcript features Jason Calacanis and his team discussing the transformative impact of **Open Claude** (formerly ClaudeBot), an orchestration platform for "agentic workflows" that uses large language models to automate complex business processes. Calacanis’s team demonstrates how they have integrated the tool into their venture firm and production company to automate guest research, booking, and internal reporting. By connecting the AI to tools like Slack, Notion, and various APIs, they have already achieved significant time savings—reducing workflows that previously took 25 hours a week down to 15, while generating more detailed research than humanly possible in a fraction of the time.

The discussion also delves into the technical and operational challenges of deploying AI agents, specifically regarding **costs and security**. Because high-volume token usage can lead to six-figure annual API fees, the team explores moving to local hosting on high-end hardware like Mac Studios to run open-source models. Security expert Rahul Sood joins the conversation to warn against "prompt injection" and malicious third-party "skills" that can compromise sensitive data. He emphasizes the need for sandboxing and cautions against giving AI agents access to sensitive accounts like password managers or primary emails, noting that these agents are essentially the "most privileged users" on a network and remain vulnerable to sophisticated cyberattacks.

The overarching theme of the episode is the **inevitable displacement of traditional roles** and the necessity of AI literacy. Calacanis and his guests argue that while "rote work" and middle-management tasks are being rapidly automated, "system thinkers" and creative individuals will see their productivity increase by as much as 100x. The conversation concludes with a sobering look at job displacement, triggered by a letter from a recently laid-off AWS employee. Calacanis asserts that we have reached a "not a drill" moment where employees must either embrace these tools to become "superhuman" or face obsolescence in an increasingly automated economy.

Today’s show: 

On Today’s action packed episode of This Week in Startups, our team is going full AI!

Jason is joined by Oliver Korzen and Lukas Durand from the LAUNCH team, alongside Rahul Sood, founder/CEO of Irrevernt Labs and founder of Voodoo PC.

With the rise of Clawdbot/Moltbot, the question on everyone’s mind is how fast, how much and how safe! Lukas and Oliver show off the ways that This Week in Startups are automating the hours of research and administrative tasks that take up our days and the what this means for running a podcast and venture firm.

Then, Rahul Sood comes on to run through the massive security risks of running a fully authorized Clawdbot, from injection attacks to terminator like behavior. But, Rahul also comes with the strategy to deploy agents in a low risk manner — keep it silo’d!

Jason and Rahul break down what this means for employment and the future of work. Jobs will get more exciting as the truly human elements left (creativity and thoughtfulness) will become be the only thing left to do. Unfortunately, this will likely leave many unemployed.
